Northwind SQL Analysis

Exploratory Data Analysis — SQL Window Functions, CTEs & Aggregations  ·  AX Consult Group 2026

Python 3 SQLite JupySQL Jupyter Notebook

Table of Contents

  1. Exploring the Database
  2. Joining Tables
  3. Employee Sales Ranking
  4. Monthly Sales & Running Total
  5. Month-over-Month Growth

1

Exploring the Database

The Northwind database models a fictional trading company. We first explore the schema to understand all eight tables and their relationships.

DATABASE SCHEMA — ENTITY RELATIONSHIP DIAGRAM

Orders sits at the centre — linking Customers, Employees and Shippers. Order_Details is the junction table between Orders and Products.

TableDescription
OrdersOrder headers: date, customer, employee, shipper, freight
Order_DetailsLine items: product, unit price, quantity, discount
CustomersCompany and contact info per customer
EmployeesEmployee records and reporting relationships
ProductsProduct catalogue with price and category
CategoriesProduct category classifications
SuppliersSupplier contact and location data
ShippersShipping company information

A simple SELECT * on customers confirms available fields:

In [ ]:
SELECT * FROM customers;
Out[ ]:
customer_id company_name contact_name contact_title address city region postal_code country phone fax

2

Joining Tables

With the schema understood, INNER JOINs combine tables into unified result sets.

2.1 — Orders + Customers

In [ ]:
SELECT c.customer_id, c.company_name, c.contact_name, c.country,
       o.order_id, o.order_date, o.freight
FROM customers AS c
INNER JOIN orders AS o
ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id;
Out[ ]:
customer_id company_name contact_name country order_id order_date freight
ALFKI Alfreds Futterkiste Maria Anders Germany 10643 1997-08-25 29.4599991
ALFKI Alfreds Futterkiste Maria Anders Germany 10692 1997-10-03 61.0200005
ALFKI Alfreds Futterkiste Maria Anders Germany 10702 1997-10-13 23.9400005
ALFKI Alfreds Futterkiste Maria Anders Germany 10835 1998-01-15 69.5299988
ALFKI Alfreds Futterkiste Maria Anders Germany 10952 1998-03-16 40.4199982
ALFKI Alfreds Futterkiste Maria Anders Germany 11011 1998-04-09 1.21000004
ANATR Ana Trujillo Emparedados y helados Ana Trujillo Mexico 10308 1996-09-18 1.61000001
ANATR Ana Trujillo Emparedados y helados Ana Trujillo Mexico 10625 1997-08-08 43.9000015
ANATR Ana Trujillo Emparedados y helados Ana Trujillo Mexico 10759 1997-11-28 11.9899998
ANATR Ana Trujillo Emparedados y helados Ana Trujillo Mexico 10926 1998-03-04 39.9199982
… 40 more rows not shown
Truncated to displaylimit of 50.

2.2 — Orders + Order Details + Products

In [ ]:
SELECT o.order_id, o.order_date,
       od.product_id, od.unit_price, od.quantity, od.discount,
       p.product_name, p.category_id
FROM orders AS o
INNER JOIN order_details AS od ON o.order_id = od.order_id
INNER JOIN products AS p ON od.product_id = p.product_id;
Out[ ]:
order_id order_date product_id unit_price quantity discount product_name category_id

10250 1996-07-08 41 7.69999981 10 0.0 Jack's New England Clam Chowder 8

In [ ]:
SELECT o.order_id, o.order_date, e.first_name, e.last_name
FROM orders AS o
INNER JOIN employees AS e ON o.employee_id = e.employee_id;
Out[ ]:

3

Employee Sales Ranking

Next we want to understand who is the top sales employee for bonuses and also who is the lowest performer.

A CTE with RANK() OVER(ORDER BY total_sales DESC) ranks all employees by discounted revenue.

In [ ]:
WITH EMPLOYEE_SALES AS (
    SELECT unit_price, quantity, o.order_id, od.order_id, o.employee_id, o.employee_id,
    SUM(unit_price*quantity)*(1-discount) AS total_sales
    FROM orders as o
    INNER JOIN order_details as od ON o.order_id = od.order_id
    INNER JOIN employees as e ON o.employee_id = e.employee_id
    GROUP by o.employee_id
)
SELECT *,
RANK() OVER (ORDER BY total_sales DESC) AS sales_rank
FROM EMPLOYEE_SALES;
Out[ ]:
unit_price quantity order_id order_id:1 employee_id employee_id:1 total_sales sales_rank
7.69999981 10 10250 10250 4 4 250187.45024599 1
15.6000004 15 10251 10251 3 3 202398.73433343708 2
12.0 20 10265 10265 2 2 177749.26048933002 3
15.1999998 50 10258 10258 1 1 161714.96763423286 4
8.0 30 10289 10289 7 7 141295.99009298 5
17.0 12 10262 10262 8 8 106640.8236467849 6
13.8999996 35 10255 10255 9 9 82963.99981972 7
18.6000004 9 10249 10249 6 6 78198.09993054 8
9.80000019 10 10248 10248 5 5 75567.74999601 9
Employee Sales Performance — light blue = top ranked, dark navy = lowest ranked

Key Findings

  • RANK() assigns rankings without gaps — ideal for leaderboard reports.
  • Sales figures account for discounts: unit_price × quantity × (1 − discount)
  • Clear performance tiers visible — useful for structuring bonus brackets.
4

Monthly Sales & Running Total

In the next step we want to calculate sales per month — joining orders and order-details.

SQLite uses strftime('%Y-%m', order_date) in place of PostgreSQL's DATE_TRUNC. A cumulative running total is produced with SUM() OVER (ORDER BY month).

In [ ]:
WITH monthly_sales AS (
    SELECT strftime('%Y-%m', order_date) AS month,
    SUM(unit_price * quantity) * (1 - discount) AS total_sales
    FROM orders AS o
    INNER JOIN order_details AS od ON od.order_id = o.order_id
    GROUP BY strftime('%Y-%m', order_date)
)
SELECT month,
SUM(total_sales) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS running_total
FROM monthly_sales
ORDER BY month;
Out[ ]:
month running_total
1996-07 30192.10019381
1996-08 56801.50027284
1996-09 84437.50040869
1996-10 125641.10043199001
1996-11 175345.10072495
1996-12 213560.15118308002
1997-01 280252.95144125
1997-02 321460.15144706
1997-03 361440.05157321
1997-04 414354.4719852954
… 13 more rows not shown
Monthly revenue (bars) with cumulative running total (red line) — Jul 1996 to May 1998

Key Findings

  • Revenue shows steady growth from mid-1996 through early 1998.
  • The running total confirms a consistent upward trajectory over the full period.
  • strftime in SQLite is equivalent to DATE_TRUNC in PostgreSQL.
5

Month-over-Month Growth

Now we want to calculate the growth month to month and year to year.

LAG() accesses the previous row's value within an ordered window — enabling percentage growth without a self-join.

In [ ]:
WITH monthly_sales AS (

    SELECT
        strftime('%Y', o.order_date) AS year,
        strftime('%m', o.order_date) AS month_num,
        strftime('%Y-%m', o.order_date) AS month,
        SUM(od.unit_price * od.quantity * (1 - od.discount)) AS total_sales

    FROM order_details od
    JOIN orders o
        ON od.order_id = o.order_id

    GROUP BY strftime('%Y-%m', o.order_date)

),

sales_with_lag AS (

    SELECT
        year,
        month_num,
        month,
        total_sales,
        LAG(total_sales) OVER (ORDER BY month) AS prev_month_sales

    FROM monthly_sales

)

SELECT
    year,
    month,
    ROUND(total_sales,2) AS total_sales,
    ROUND(prev_month_sales,2) AS prev_month_sales,
    ROUND(total_sales - prev_month_sales,2) AS growth_amount,
    ROUND(((total_sales - prev_month_sales) * 100.0 / prev_month_sales),2) AS growth_percent

FROM sales_with_lag
ORDER BY month;
Out[ ]:
year month total_sales prev_month_sales growth_amount growth_percent
1996 1996-07 27861.9 None None None
1996 1996-08 25485.28 27861.9 -2376.62 -8.53
1996 1996-09 26381.4 25485.28 896.13 3.52
1996 1996-10 37515.72 26381.4 11134.32 42.21
1996 1996-11 45600.05 37515.72 8084.32 21.55
1996 1996-12 45239.63 45600.05 -360.41 -0.79
1997 1997-01 61258.07 45239.63 16018.44 35.41
1997 1997-02 38483.63 61258.07 -22774.44 -37.18
1997 1997-03 38547.22 38483.63 63.59 0.17
1997 1997-04 53032.95 38547.22 14485.73 37.58
… 13 more rows not shown
Month-over-month sales growth % — blue = positive months, red = negative months

Does not seem like there is serious seasonality in the data.

Key Findings

  • LAG() looks back one row within the ordered window to access the previous period.
  • Growth formula: (current − previous) / previous × 100
  • No strong seasonal pattern detected over the observed period.
6

Customer Segmentation

We would like to identify customers with higher sales values so we can market to them or assign key account managers.

AVG() OVER() calculates the global average inline, enabling per-row threshold comparison via CASE WHEN.

In [ ]:
WITH OrderValues AS (
    SELECT 
        o.customer_id,
        o.order_id,
        SUM(od.unit_price * od.quantity * (1 - od.discount)) AS order_value
    FROM orders AS o
    JOIN order_details AS od
        ON o.order_id = od.order_id
    GROUP BY o.customer_id, o.order_id
)
SELECT 
    customer_id,
    order_id,
    ROUND(order_value, 2) AS order_value,
    CASE
        WHEN order_value > AVG(order_value) OVER () THEN 'Above Average'
        ELSE 'Below Average'
    END AS value_category
FROM OrderValues
ORDER BY order_value DESC;
Out[ ]:
customer_id order_id order_value value_category
QUICK 10865 16387.5 Above Average
HANAR 10981 15810.0 Above Average
SAVEA 11030 12615.05 Above Average
RATTC 10889 11380.0 Above Average
SIMOB 10417 11188.4 Above Average
KOENE 10817 10952.84 Above Average
HUNGO 10897 10835.24 Above Average
RATTC 10479 10495.6 Above Average
QUICK 10540 10191.7 Above Average
QUICK 10691 10164.8 Above Average
… 40 more rows not shown
Truncated to displaylimit of 50.
Customer segmentation by average order value — above vs below average split

Key Findings

  • High-value accounts are prime candidates for dedicated key account manager assignment.
  • AVG() OVER() returns the global average alongside every row — no subquery needed.
  • CASE WHEN … ELSE … END combined with window functions enables threshold-based classification.
7

Category & Product Sales

We now want to turn our attention to the most profitable product categories and their share of total sales.

A CTE calculates each category's revenue share. Then ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category_id ORDER BY total_sales DESC) filters the top 3 products per category.

7.1 — Category Revenue Share

In [ ]:
WITH CategorySales AS (
    SELECT Categories.Category_ID, Categories.Category_Name,
           SUM(Products.Unit_Price * Quantity * (1 - Discount)) AS "Total Sales"
    FROM Categories
    JOIN Products ON Categories.Category_ID = Products.Category_ID
    JOIN Order_Details ON Products.Product_ID = Order_Details.Product_ID
    GROUP BY Categories.Category_ID
)
SELECT Category_ID, Category_Name,
       "Total Sales" / SUM("Total Sales") OVER () * 100 AS "Sales Percentage"
FROM CategorySales;
Out[ ]:
Category_ID Category_Name Sales Percentage
1 Beverages 21.331025404425716
2 Condiments 8.400470714263516
3 Confections 13.28973740810202
4 Dairy Products 18.556754764962903
5 Grains/Cereals 7.51047348230901
6 Meat/Poultry 12.902483712489019
7 Produce 7.813322139710293
8 Seafood 10.195732373737526
Revenue share (%) by product category

Beverages account for almost 21% of all sales (Category 1), followed by Dairy Products at almost 18% (Category 4).

7.2 — Top 3 Products per Category

In [ ]:
WITH ProductSales AS (
    SELECT Products.Category_ID, 
           Products.Product_ID, Products.Product_Name,
           SUM(Products.Unit_Price * Quantity * (1 - Discount)) AS "Total Sales"
    FROM Products
    JOIN Order_Details ON Products.Product_ID = Order_Details.Product_ID
    GROUP BY Products.Category_ID, Products.Product_ID
)
SELECT Category_ID, 
       Product_ID, Product_Name,
       "Total Sales"
FROM (
    SELECT Category_ID, 
           Product_ID, Product_Name,
           "Total Sales", 
           ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY Category_ID ORDER BY "Total Sales" DESC) AS rn
    FROM ProductSales
) tmp
WHERE rn <= 3;
Out[ ]:
Category_ID Product_ID Product_Name Total Sales
1 38 Côte de Blaye 153897.17488998876
1 43 Ipoh Coffee 25109.099973596
1 2 Chang 17719.399970626
2 63 Vegie-spread 18343.6156018398
2 61 Sirop d'érable 15022.3499609265
2 65 Louisiana Fiery Hot Pepper Sauce 14893.92692093847
3 62 Tarte au sucre 50737.09413235931
3 20 Sir Rodney's Marmalade 24199.5599875179
3 26 Gumbär Gummibärchen 21662.689117650876
4 59 Raclette Courdavault 76683.7499002465
… 14 more rows not shown
Block size = total sales value  ·  Dark navy = highest revenue, light blue = lower revenue

Key Findings

  • Beverages (~21%) and Dairy Products (~18%) are the top two revenue contributors.
  • Côte de Blaye ($153,897) and Thüringer Rostbratwurst ($84,784) are the highest individual products.
  • ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY category_id) restarts ranking within each category for top-N filtering.
